assertion_results.text: The plan explicitly references reusing common/src/db/query.rs::apply_filter for comma-separated multi-value query parameter parsing rather than writing new parsing logic (constraint 5.4)

The apply_filter function already handles both single-value and multi-value comma-separated parameters, so no new parsing logic is needed.' The Reuse Summary table also lists apply_filter with 'Called directly in PackageService::list() to parse comma-separated license values and generate SQL IN clause -- no new parsing code written.'

assertion_results.text: The plan references following the filter pattern from modules/fundamental/src/advisory/endpoints/list.rs as a structural guide for implementing the license filter

assertion_results.text: The plan references using entity/src/package_license.rs for the JOIN query rather than writing raw SQL or creating a new entity
assertion_results.evidence: The plan states in the service/mod.rs changes: 'add a JOIN to the package_license table using the SeaORM entity defined in entity/src/package_license.rs (Reuse Candidate 3). This avoids writing raw SQL and leverages the existing entity relationship definitions.' The reuse-analysis.md further states: 'No raw SQL is written -- the SeaORM entity relations handle the JOIN condition. No new migration is needed.'

assertion_results.text: The reuse analysis covers all 3 Reuse Candidates from the task description, explaining how each would be applied
assertion_results.evidence: The reuse-analysis.md has three detailed sections: (1) 'Reuse Candidate 1: common/src/db/query.rs::apply_filter' with What it provides, How it would be reused (including code example), and Why reuse is critical; (2) 'Reuse Candidate 2: modules/fundamental/src/advisory/endpoints/list.rs (severity filter pattern)' with the same structure; (3) 'Reuse Candidate 3: entity/src/package_license.rs' with the same structure.
